Party_Details.Name: Jurong Readymix Concrete Pte Ltd

Facts.Text: Jurong Readymix Concrete Pte Ltd was a supplier of concrete and entered into a supply arrangement with Boonann Construction Pte Ltd evidenced by a letter dated 6 February 1996.

On 6 February 1996, Jurong Readymix Concrete Pte Ltd issued a letter to Boonann Construction Pte Ltd setting out terms for the supply of readymixed concrete, including a credit term of 45 days from the date of delivery and a late payment interest of 2% per month on overdue bills.

The 6 February 1996 supply letter from Jurong Readymix Concrete Pte Ltd to Boonann Construction Pte Ltd incorporated printed terms including clause 14 providing for late payment interest of 2% per month on overdue bills.
Boonann Construction Pte Ltd had an established credit limit of $60,000 with Jurong Readymix Concrete Pte Ltd arising from previous dealings.
In or about April 1996, it was anticipated that Boonann Construction Pte Ltd's orders for concrete would exceed the credit limit of $60,000.
In or about April 1996, management informed the contractors that if they wanted the credit limit to be extended to $1m, a guarantee was required as security.

On 8 April 1996, a letter of guarantee on the letterhead of Kaki Bukit Industrial Park Pte Ltd, signed by director Chng Heng Tiu, stated that if Boonann Construction Pte Ltd failed to honour payment for the supply of readymixed concrete to the proposed Kaki Bukit Industrial Park project, Kaki Bukit Industrial Park Pte Ltd would make direct payment to Jurong Readymix Concrete Pte Ltd.

The 8 April 1996 letter of guarantee from Kaki Bukit Industrial Park Pte Ltd to Jurong Readymix Concrete Pte Ltd referenced the supply of readymixed concrete for the proposed Kaki Bukit Industrial Park project and was signed by director Chng Heng Tiu.
The 8 April 1996 letter of guarantee addressed to Jurong Readymix Concrete Pte Ltd was prepared by a representative of the project.

After receiving the guarantee dated 8 April 1996, Jurong Readymix Concrete Pte Ltd issued an authorised price letter to Boonann Construction Pte Ltd setting out prices for concrete to be supplied and stating a credit limit of $1m.
Following the issuance of the guarantee, Jurong Readymix Concrete Pte Ltd supplied readymixed concrete ordered by Boonann Construction Pte Ltd.

The printed terms of the 6 February 1996 supply letter from Jurong Readymix Concrete Pte Ltd to Boonann Construction Pte Ltd included clause 17 reserving to Jurong Readymix Concrete Pte Ltd the right to stop supplies without notice.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether a corporate guarantor is liable for unpaid supplies under a written guarantee provided in exchange for an increased credit limit, and whether continued supplies to the principal debtor constitute a variation that discharges the guarantee.
Party_Details.Rule: A guarantee is enforceable if supported by valid consideration, such as the extension of credit to a third party. A guarantor is only discharged from liability if the underlying contract is materially varied without their consent in a manner that prejudices the guarantor's position.
Party_Details.Application: A concrete supplier increased a contractor's credit limit from $60,000 to $1,000,000 upon receiving a written guarantee from the project developer. The developer subsequently argued that the guarantee was not supported by consideration and that the supplier's decision to continue providing concrete despite payment delays constituted a variation of the original agreement that should release the guarantor from its obligations.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: It was determined that the extension of the credit limit to $1,000,000 constituted valid consideration for the guarantee. The supplier's continued fulfillment of orders and the decision not to immediately stop supplies were within the terms of the original supply arrangement and did not amount to a material contractual variation. Consequently, the guarantee remained valid and enforceable, and the developer was held liable for the outstanding balance of $610,270.91.

Party_Details.Name: Kaki Bukit Industrial Park Pte Ltd

Facts.Text: Kaki Bukit Industrial Park Pte Ltd was incorporated in 1994 with its main object being the development of the Kaki Bukit Industrial Park.
Ho Kok Cheong acted as project manager of Kaki Bukit Industrial Park Pte Ltd for the Kaki Bukit Industrial Park development.
Ho Kok Cheong had long-standing involvement in the construction industry but his personal involvement in construction companies was restricted due to being an undischarged bankrupt.
The project was initiated by family members who invited Chng Heng Tiu to participate as an investor and director to provide necessary funding.
Chng Heng Tiu brought in Chwee Meng Chong to participate in the Kaki Bukit Industrial Park project.
The company’s board was split between two primary groups: the Chng-Chwee group and the Ho family group.
Chng Heng Tiu held the majority of shares in Kaki Bukit Industrial Park Pte Ltd through his personal holding company.
The directors of Kaki Bukit Industrial Park Pte Ltd were Chng Heng Tiu, Chwee Meng Chong, Ho Mun Sang and Ho Mun Fei.

On 8 April 1996, Kaki Bukit Industrial Park Pte Ltd issued a letter on its letterhead to Jurong Readymix Concrete Pte Ltd stating that if Boonann Construction Pte Ltd failed to honour payment for the supply of readymixed concrete, the company would make direct payment.
The 8 April 1996 letter of guarantee was signed by director Chng Heng Tiu.
The 8 April 1996 guarantee letter was prepared by a representative of the contractor.
Before signing the 8 April 1996 guarantee, director Chng Heng Tiu consulted only one other director and did not notify the Ho family directors.
The guarantee was treated by the signing director as a matter of day-to-day construction operations within his authority.
The guarantee exposed the company to a potential liability of up to $1,000,000.
The signing director relied on his majority shareholding and casting vote as justification for proceeding without full board consensus.
The signing director did not obtain or review the underlying supply contract between the concrete supplier and the contractor before executing the guarantee.
The company did not obtain legal advice on the terms or implications of the guarantee before it was signed.
The signing director proceeded on the assumption that if the contractor defaulted, the company could offset the payments against progress amounts due to the contractor.
Administrative details of the guarantee were treated as minor matters by the majority shareholders.
The company's memorandum of association did not expressly exclude or modify the statutory power to provide corporate guarantees.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether a company is bound by a guarantee executed by its majority-shareholder director without a formal board resolution, and whether the company is entitled to be indemnified by that director for the resulting loss.
Party_Details.Rule: A company possesses the statutory power to issue guarantees unless explicitly restricted by its constitution. Under company law, directors must exercise reasonable diligence in managing company affairs. If a director's failure to consult the board or review material contracts leads to corporate loss, the company may be entitled to recover those losses from the director personally.
Party_Details.Application: The company's majority-shareholder director signed a $1,000,000 guarantee to a supplier without seeking a board resolution or reviewing the underlying supply terms. The company's constitution did not bar such guarantees. When the contractor defaulted, the supplier enforced the guarantee against the company. The company then sought to hold the director personally responsible for the liability, arguing that his unilateral and poorly researched decision was a breach of his duty of diligence.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The company was found to have the legal capacity to issue the guarantee, as its memorandum of association did not exclude statutory powers. However, the signing director was found to have failed to exercise reasonable diligence by failing to consult the full board, failing to seek legal advice, and failing to review the underlying supply contract which may have offered alternative protections. While the company was liable to the supplier under the guarantee, it was entitled to be indemnified by the director for the principal amount because the loss was a direct result of his negligent decision-making process.

Party_Details.Name: Chng Heng Tiu

Facts.Text: Chng Heng Tiu was one of the directors of Kaki Bukit Industrial Park Pte Ltd.
Chng Heng Tiu held the majority of the shares in the company through his personal holding company.
Chng Heng Tiu brought Chwee Meng Chong into the venture.
On 1 December 1997, Chng Heng Tiu and associated partners contracted to sell their shares to another corporate entity.
On 8 April 1996, Chng Heng Tiu signed a guarantee on the company's letterhead ensuring direct payment to a concrete supplier if the contractor defaulted.
The guarantee was signed without a formal board meeting and without the knowledge of the minority directors.
The director categorized the guarantee as a routine operational matter within his authority to decide independently.
At the time of execution, the director was aware the guarantee reached a value of $1m.
The director intended to use his casting vote to override any board opposition had the matter been raised formally.
The director did not request or read the specific supply contract he was guaranteeing.
The director did not seek professional legal counsel regarding the risks of the guarantee.
The director acted on the unconfirmed assumption that the company could recover any guarantee payouts by deducting them from the contractor's future earnings.
Procedural compliance was viewed as a minor detail due to the director's majority control of the company.
A representative of the contractor suggested that the issuance of the guarantee would lead to the installation of an on-site batching plant, aiding project completion.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether a director breaches his statutory duty to act with reasonable diligence by unilaterally signing a substantial corporate guarantee without board consultation, contract review, or legal advice.
Party_Details.Rule: Under section 157 of the Companies Act, directors are required to act honestly and use reasonable diligence in the discharge of their duties. A director who exposes a company to significant financial risk through a lack of proper inquiry or procedural oversight may be held personally liable to indemnify the company for its losses.
Party_Details.Application: A majority-shareholder director executed a $1,000,000 corporate guarantee to a supplier. He did not notify the full board, did not read the terms of the primary supply agreement, and did not seek legal advice on the company's exposure. He relied solely on his controlling position and unverified assumptions about the company's ability to offset the debt. When the contractor defaulted and the company was forced to pay the supplier, the director's diligence was questioned.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: It was held that the director failed to exercise the reasonable diligence required by law. His failure to consult his co-directors, his lack of inquiry into the supply contract, and the absence of legal review constituted a breach of his statutory duties. Because these failures directly resulted in the company's financial exposure to the supplier, the director was ordered to personally indemnify the company for the principal sum of the loss. His reliance on his majority voting power did not excuse him from the fundamental duty to act with care and diligence.
